brief:
The single most important signal this week is not what moved — it is what didn't. The entire yield curve sat perfectly still, with **zero basis points** of change across every tenor from 91-day bills to 20-year bonds. In a market where CPI is running at **9.13%**, the repo rate sits at **10%**, and fiscal slippage is becoming structurally embedded, this kind of stasis is not calm — it is complacency. The curve itself tells a story that deserves scrutiny: the front end is reasonably orderly, with 91-day bills at **9.85%** and 182-day at **10.2%**, both sitting just below or at the repo rate, reflecting comfortable overnight liquidity with call money anchored at **9.8%**. But then the curve develops a notable kink — the 364-day bill at **10.07%** actually trades *below* the 182-day, a mild inversion that suggests either technical demand at the one-year point or, more likely, the market pricing in a rate cut within the next twelve months that it does not yet expect within six. Move further out and the picture becomes genuinely distorted: the **2-year bond at 11.95%** sits a full **80 basis points above** the 10-year at **11.15%**, and the **15-year at 12.68%** trades a remarkable **103 basis points** over the 20-year at **11.65%**. This is not a normal term structure. The belly is being punished relative to the wings, and the 15-year point is screaming supply indigestion or a buyer strike — a signal we cannot afford to ignore.

The structural backdrop explains why the market is frozen but tense. Excess liquidity at **৳1.9 lakh crore** is ample by any historical measure, and the ADR at **75.8%** tells us the banking system is not stretching for credit — there is room to absorb government paper. Yet absorption has its limits. Revenue collection at just **44.9% of the FY26 target** with barely three months left in the fiscal year virtually guarantees an acceleration of government borrowing in Q4. ADP implementation at a dismal **11.5%** means spending has been back-loaded, and when it arrives, it will arrive alongside heavier T-bill and bond issuance. The **10-year auction on March 8** is the canary here: a bid-cover of only **1.4x** with **10% devolvement** on Bangladesh Bank tells us the market is already resisting duration at these yields. Compare that to the 91-day auction's healthy **1.92x** cover with zero devolvement, and the message is clear — banks are happy to park money short but are demanding a concession for anything beyond five years that the government is not yet willing to pay. Total debt at **৳21.5 lakh crore**, representing **40% of GDP**, with interest consuming **22% of revenue**, frames a fiscal trajectory where the sovereign's borrowing cost is becoming self-reinforcing. With Brent at **$78.4** and JKM LNG at **$12.8/mmbtu**, the energy import bill provides no relief to the external account, though reserves at **$35.1 billion** and a relatively stable exchange rate of **৳121.2** suggest the external front is manageable for now.

Here is the call. The 2-year point at **11.95%** — nearly **200 basis points** above the repo rate and **80 basis points** above the 10-year — represents the most compelling relative value on the curve, but only if you believe BB holds rates steady or cuts within twelve months; at current CPI levels, that is not a certainty, so size the position accordingly. I would be actively adding 2-year and modestly building 5-year at **10.82%**, where the **1.6x** bid-cover and zero devolvement confirm genuine demand. The 15-year at **12.68%** looks optically attractive but is a trap until we see improved auction dynamics at that tenor — the illiquidity premium is real and exits will be painful. On the short end, rolling 91-day bills at **9.85%** earns you negative real carry against **9.13%** inflation on a pre-tax basis, but it preserves optionality heading into what promises to be a volatile Q4 borrowing calendar. The key catalyst to watch is the next long-bond auction: if 10-year devolvement persists above **10%** or bid-cover slips below **1.3x**, it will confirm that the market is demanding a repricing that the Ministry of Finance has so far resisted. When that dam breaks, it will reprice the entire belly violently, and we want to be positioned in tenors where demand is proven, not where it is hoped for.
